title: Git & GitHub Cheatsheet
description: The complete practical Git & GitHub reference — from first commit to advanced team workflows, real-world recovery, and open source contribution. Bookmark-worthy.

concepts.name: Install & Configure Git
concepts.explanation: Before anything else, tell Git who you are. Every commit you make will carry your name and email — like a signature on every piece of work. This config lives in a global file (~/.gitconfig) so you only set it once per machine.
concepts.code:
# Install (one-time per machine)
# macOS: brew install git
# Linux: sudo apt install git
# Windows: https://git-scm.com/download/win

# Set your identity (REQUIRED before first commit)
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@example.com"

# Set default branch name to 'main' (modern standard)
git config --global init.defaultBranch main

# Set VS Code as default editor
git config --global core.editor "code --wait"

# Verify your config
git config --list
keyPoints: --global applies config to all repos on your machine; --local applies to one repo only
user.email should match your GitHub account email for commit attribution
init.defaultBranch main avoids the old 'master' default
git config --list shows all active config values (global + local merged)

concepts.name: Initialize a Repository
concepts.explanation: git init creates a hidden .git/ folder that IS your repository — it stores every version, every branch, every commit. Without it, Git knows nothing about your project. You only run this once per project.
concepts.code:
# Start tracking an existing folder
cd my-project
git init

# Or create a new folder and init in one step
git init my-project
cd my-project

# Check what Git created
ls -la  # You'll see a .git/ directory

# See current repository status
git status
keyPoints: The .git/ folder contains everything — branches, history, config, objects
Never manually edit files inside .git/ — you can corrupt the repo
git status is your most-used command — run it constantly
An empty repo has no commits yet; nothing is tracked until you add files

concepts.name: Clone an Existing Repository
concepts.explanation: Cloning downloads a complete copy of a remote repo — all history, all branches — to your local machine. It also automatically sets up the remote connection called 'origin' pointing back to where you cloned from.
concepts.code:
# Clone via HTTPS (no SSH setup needed)
git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git

# Clone into a specific folder name
git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git my-folder

# Clone a specific branch only
git clone -b develop https://github.com/user/repo.git

# Clone with limited history (faster for large repos)
git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/user/repo.git

# After cloning, see what remote was configured
git remote -v
keyPoints: HTTPS clone works everywhere but asks for credentials on push
SSH clone (git@github.com:user/repo.git) never asks for password once set up
--depth 1 creates a 'shallow clone' with only the latest snapshot, not full history
origin is just a nickname for the remote URL — you can rename it

concepts.name: The .gitignore File
concepts.explanation: A .gitignore tells Git which files to completely ignore — never track, never commit. Crucial for keeping secrets out of version control and keeping your repo clean of build artifacts and editor junk.
concepts.code:
# Create .gitignore in project root
touch .gitignore

# Common .gitignore patterns:

# Ignore a specific file
.env
secrets.txt

# Ignore entire folders
node_modules/
.gradle/
target/
build/
dist/

# Ignore by file extension
*.log
*.class
*.jar
*.pyc

# Ignore but keep the folder itself
logs/
!logs/.gitkeep

# Check if a file is being ignored
git check-ignore -v filename.txt

# Force-add an ignored file (use carefully!)
git add -f accidentally-ignored.txt
keyPoints: Add .gitignore BEFORE you first commit — retroactively ignoring tracked files requires extra steps
Use gitignore.io to generate templates for your language/framework
Once a file is tracked, adding it to .gitignore won't stop tracking — run: git rm --cached filename
Never commit .env, API keys, passwords, or credentials — even private repos can be exposed

concepts.name: Understanding the Three Areas
concepts.explanation: Git has three distinct areas your files live in. Misunderstanding this is the source of 90% of beginner confusion. Think of it as a workflow: Working Directory → Staging Area → Repository.
concepts.code:
# 1. Working Directory = your actual files on disk
#    (untracked or modified but not staged)

# 2. Staging Area (Index) = files you've marked for next commit
#    git add moves files here

# 3. Repository (.git) = permanent commit history
#    git commit moves staged files here

# See the status of all three areas
git status

# See what changed in working directory (not staged)
git diff

# See what's staged and ready to commit
git diff --staged

# See everything at once
git diff HEAD
keyPoints: git add = 'I want to include this change in my next commit'
git commit = 'Save everything I've staged as a permanent snapshot'
You can stage only part of a file: git add -p filename (interactive patch staging)
git diff (no args) shows unstaged changes; git diff --staged shows staged changes

concepts.name: Staging and Committing
concepts.explanation: The daily bread of Git work. Stage what you want included, then commit with a clear message. A great commit message is a gift to your future self (and teammates) trying to understand what changed and why.
concepts.code:
# Stage specific file
git add filename.txt

# Stage multiple files
git add file1.js file2.js

# Stage all changes in current directory
git add .

# Stage parts of a file interactively
git add -p filename.js

# Commit with message
git commit -m "feat: add user login validation"

# Stage all tracked files AND commit in one step
git commit -am "fix: correct null check in parser"

# Open editor for longer commit message
git commit
keyPoints: git add . stages everything — double-check with git status first
git commit -am only stages TRACKED files — new untracked files still need git add
Commit messages in imperative mood: 'Add feature' not 'Added feature'
Use Conventional Commits prefix: feat:, fix:, docs:, refactor:, test:, chore:

concepts.name: Viewing History (git log)
concepts.explanation: git log is your time machine. It shows every commit: what changed, who changed it, when. The formatting options let you scan history at a glance or dig deep into specific changes.
concepts.code:
# Default log (too verbose for daily use)
git log

# Compact one-line view
git log --oneline

# Visual branch graph
git log --oneline --graph --all --decorate

# Show commits by specific author
git log --author="Alice"

# Show commits on a specific file
git log --oneline -- src/auth/login.js

# Show what changed in each commit
git log -p

# Show last 5 commits
git log -5 --oneline

# Search commit messages
git log --grep="login"

# Show commits between two dates
git log --after="2024-01-01" --before="2024-06-01" --oneline
keyPoints: --graph --all shows entire repo history including all branches as a visual tree
-p (patch) shows the actual diff for each commit — great for code archaeology
Each commit has a SHA-1 hash (like a8f3c2d) — use first 7 chars to reference it
git log --follow -p -- old/path shows history even after file was renamed/moved

concepts.name: Amending & Rewriting Last Commit
concepts.explanation: Typo in your commit message? Forgot to include a file? git commit --amend lets you fix the most recent commit before you've pushed it. This rewrites history — safe for local commits, dangerous for pushed ones.
concepts.code:
# Fix the last commit message only
git commit --amend -m "correct: fixed the actual bug"

# Add a forgotten file to the last commit
git add forgotten-file.js
git commit --amend --no-edit  # Keep the same message

# Change both files AND message
git add extra-fix.js
git commit --amend -m "feat: add login + validation"

# WARNING: Don't amend if you've already pushed!
# If you must, force push (only on your own branch):
git push --force-with-lease origin feature/my-branch
keyPoints: --amend creates a NEW commit replacing the old one — the original SHA changes
--no-edit keeps the existing message while amending file content
--force-with-lease is safer than --force: it fails if someone else pushed since you last fetched
NEVER amend commits on shared branches like main/develop — it resets others' history

concepts.name: Viewing File Changes (git diff)
concepts.explanation: Before you commit, always review exactly what you're about to save. git diff is how you see the precise line-by-line changes. Green lines (with +) are additions; red lines (with -) are deletions.
concepts.code:
# What changed but isn't staged yet
git diff

# What is staged and will go into next commit
git diff --staged
# OR: git diff --cached (same thing)

# Compare two commits
git diff abc123 def456

# Compare current code to last commit
git diff HEAD

# Diff a specific file only
git diff HEAD -- src/utils.js

# Compare two branches
git diff main..feature/my-feature

# Just show filenames that changed (no line details)
git diff --name-only main..feature-branch
keyPoints: git diff (no args) = working directory vs staging area
git diff HEAD = working directory vs last commit (everything uncommitted)
git diff main..feature shows what feature adds that main doesn't have
Use git difftool to open diffs in a visual tool like VS Code or meld

concepts.name: Removing & Moving Files
concepts.explanation: Use git rm and git mv instead of regular shell rm/mv. This way Git tracks the deletion or rename in the staging area automatically — no need to manually stage the change afterward.
concepts.code:
# Remove file from working directory AND staging area
git rm filename.txt

# Remove from staging only (stop tracking, keep file on disk)
git rm --cached filename.txt

# Remove all .log files from tracking
git rm --cached *.log

# Rename/move a file
git mv old-name.js new-name.js
git mv src/utils.js src/helpers/utils.js

# Commit the removal or rename
git commit -m "refactor: rename utils to helpers"
keyPoints: git rm --cached = 'stop tracking this file but don't delete it from disk' (useful after forgetting .gitignore)
git mv is equivalent to: mv old new && git rm old && git add new
Git detects renames automatically if ~50%+ of content is similar — shown in git log as 'rename'
After git rm --cached, add the file to .gitignore so it stays untracked

concepts.name: git stash — Save Work Without Committing
concepts.explanation: You're mid-feature when something urgent needs fixing on main. You can't commit half-done work. git stash saves your changes to a temporary stack so you can switch context instantly and return later.
concepts.code:
# Stash current changes (tracked files)
git stash

# Stash with a descriptive name
git stash save "WIP: half-done user dashboard"

# Stash including untracked files
git stash -u

# List all stashes
git stash list
# Output: stash@{0}: WIP on main: abc123 feat: add login

# Apply most recent stash (keeps it in list)
git stash apply

# Apply AND remove from list
git stash pop

# Apply specific stash
git stash apply stash@{2}

# Delete a specific stash
git stash drop stash@{1}

# Clear all stashes
git stash clear
keyPoints: Stashes are LIFO (last in, first out) — stash@{0} is always the most recent
-u (--include-untracked) stashes new files too — without it, only tracked changes are saved
git stash pop = apply + drop in one step; prefer apply when unsure if it'll conflict
Stashes are local-only — they don't push to the remote

concepts.name: Creating & Switching Branches
concepts.explanation: Branches are Git's killer feature. They let you work on features, bugs, and experiments completely in isolation without affecting working code. Branches are just pointers to commits — they're practically free to create.
concepts.code:
# List all local branches (* = current)
git branch

# List all branches including remote
git branch -a

# Create a new branch (stays on current branch)
git branch feature/user-auth

# Create AND switch to it (classic way)
git checkout -b feature/user-auth

# Modern preferred way (Git 2.23+)
git switch -c feature/user-auth

# Switch to existing branch
git switch main
# or legacy: git checkout main

# Rename a branch
git branch -m old-name new-name

# Delete a merged branch
git branch -d feature/done

# Force-delete an unmerged branch
git branch -D feature/abandoned
keyPoints: git switch is the modern command; git checkout does too many things (switching, restoring files, etc.)
Branch from the right base: switch to main first, then create your feature branch
-d (lowercase) only deletes merged branches — safe; -D (uppercase) force deletes regardless
Branch naming conventions: feature/, fix/, hotfix/, release/, chore/

concepts.name: Git Flow — the Classic Branching Strategy
concepts.explanation: Git Flow is a strict branching model that defines roles for each branch. Perfect for teams with scheduled releases. Two eternal branches (main, develop) plus supporting branches for features, releases, and hotfixes.
concepts.code:
# Git Flow branch types:
# main      → production-ready, always stable
# develop   → integration branch for features
# feature/* → new features (branch from develop)
# release/* → release preparation (from develop)
# hotfix/*  → critical production fixes (from main)

# Typical feature workflow in Git Flow:

# 1. Start feature
git switch develop
git pull origin develop
git switch -c feature/shopping-cart

# 2. Work, commit, work, commit...
git commit -m "feat: add cart item model"

# 3. Finish feature
git switch develop
git merge --no-ff feature/shopping-cart
git push origin develop
git branch -d feature/shopping-cart

# --no-ff = No fast-forward: preserves branch history as a merge commit
keyPoints: --no-ff creates a merge commit even if fast-forward is possible — keeps branch history visible
Hotfix branches come from main so you fix production without pulling in unready develop code
Release branches allow last-minute testing and version bumping without blocking new feature work
Use 'git flow' CLI tool to automate these workflows: brew install git-flow-avh

concepts.name: Trunk-Based Development
concepts.explanation: The modern alternative to Git Flow used by Google, Meta, and most high-velocity teams. Everyone commits to main (trunk) frequently — at least once a day. Short-lived feature branches (1-2 days max) are merged quickly via PRs. Feature flags control release of incomplete features.
concepts.code:
# Philosophy: commit to main often, use feature flags
# No long-lived branches

# Day 1 morning: branch from fresh main
git switch main
git pull origin main
git switch -c feature/add-search  # lives < 2 days!

# Work in small chunks
git commit -m "feat: add search input component"
git commit -m "feat: wire search to API endpoint"

# Day 1 afternoon: merge back quickly
git push origin feature/add-search
# Open PR → Quick code review → Merge

# Feature not ready for users? Use a feature flag:
# if (featureFlags.newSearch) { <NewSearch /> } else { <OldSearch /> }
keyPoints: The key rule: no branch lives longer than 2 days — this forces small, reviewable changes
Feature flags (feature toggles) let you deploy code without releasing the feature to users
CI tests run on every commit to main — broken main = team stops and fixes immediately
Requires high test coverage and CI/CD pipeline — not ideal for teams without these

concepts.name: GitHub Flow — Simple & Effective
concepts.explanation: GitHub's own recommended workflow. Simpler than Git Flow, more structured than pure trunk-based. Perfect for teams that deploy continuously. One long-lived branch (main), feature branches for all work, PRs for code review.
concepts.code:
# The 6 steps of GitHub Flow:

# 1. Branch from main
git switch main && git pull
git switch -c fix/broken-login

# 2. Make commits
git commit -m "fix: handle empty email in login form"
git commit -m "test: add coverage for empty email case"

# 3. Push to GitHub
git push -u origin fix/broken-login

# 4. Open a Pull Request on GitHub (UI or CLI)
# gh pr create --title "Fix broken login" --body "..."

# 5. Review + CI passes → Merge PR
# (squash merge or merge commit, per team preference)

# 6. Delete the branch + deploy
git switch main
git pull origin main
git branch -d fix/broken-login
keyPoints: Main is always deployable — every merge to main can go to production
Deploy from the feature branch BEFORE merging to catch production bugs
This is the most widely used workflow for open source and web projects
Combine with branch protection rules: require PR review + CI green before merge

concepts.name: git merge — Combining Branches
concepts.explanation: Merge integrates changes from one branch into another. Git has two merge strategies: fast-forward (just moves the pointer, no extra commit) and a true merge commit (creates a new commit with two parents, preserving branch history).
concepts.code:
# Basic merge: integrate feature into main
git switch main
git merge feature/user-auth

# Force a merge commit even if fast-forward is possible
git merge --no-ff feature/user-auth

# Squash all feature commits into ONE commit on main
git merge --squash feature/user-auth
git commit -m "feat: complete user authentication"

# Abort a merge that's gone wrong
git merge --abort

# Check if a branch has been merged
git branch --merged main    # branches already in main
git branch --no-merged main # branches NOT in main

# See the merge commit graph
git log --oneline --graph --all
keyPoints: Fast-forward merge: happens when there are no diverging commits — branch pointer just moves forward
--no-ff always creates a merge commit — better for traceability in shared branches
--squash combines all branch commits into one staged change — cleaner history but loses individual commit details
Merge conflicts happen when the same lines were changed differently in both branches

concepts.name: git rebase — Cleaner Linear History
concepts.explanation: Rebase replays your commits on top of another branch's latest commit. The result looks like you branched from the latest code — a clean, linear history with no merge commits. Think of it as 'reattaching' your branch to a new starting point.
concepts.code:
# You're on feature/dashboard, main has moved ahead
# BEFORE: 
#   main: A - B - C
#   feature: A - B - D - E

git switch feature/dashboard
git rebase main

# AFTER: D and E are replayed on top of C
#   main: A - B - C
#   feature: A - B - C - D' - E'  (D' and E' are new commits)

# Interactive rebase: reorder, squash, edit commits
git rebase -i HEAD~3  # Opens editor for last 3 commits
# Commands in editor:
# pick  = keep commit as-is
# reword = change commit message
# squash = combine with previous commit
# fixup  = squash, discard its message
# drop   = remove this commit entirely

# Abort a rebase in progress
git rebase --abort

# Continue after resolving conflict
git rebase --continue
keyPoints: Rebase rewrites history — new commits have new SHA hashes (hence D' and E')
GOLDEN RULE: Never rebase commits that have been pushed to a shared branch
Interactive rebase (-i) is your 'clean up history before PR' superpower
git rebase vs merge: rebase for local cleanup; merge for integrating into shared branches

concepts.name: Cherry-Pick — Grab Specific Commits
concepts.explanation: Cherry-pick applies a single commit from any branch onto your current branch. Perfect for backporting a hotfix to an older release, or pulling in just one commit from a colleague's WIP branch without taking everything.
concepts.code:
# Copy one specific commit to current branch
git cherry-pick abc1234

# Cherry-pick multiple commits
git cherry-pick abc1234 def5678

# Cherry-pick a range of commits
git cherry-pick abc1234..def5678

# Cherry-pick without immediately committing
git cherry-pick -n abc1234  # Stages changes, you commit manually

# Real scenario: backport hotfix to v1.x branch
git switch release/v1.x
git cherry-pick abc1234   # The commit that fixed the bug on main
git push origin release/v1.x

# If conflict during cherry-pick
git add resolved-file.js
git cherry-pick --continue
# Or bail out:
git cherry-pick --abort
keyPoints: Cherry-pick creates a COPY of the commit with a new SHA — not the same commit object
-n (--no-commit) stages the changes but lets YOU control when to commit
Cherry-picking many commits is often a sign you should use merge or rebase instead
Great use case: bug fix on main needs to also go into release/v2.1 maintenance branch

concepts.name: Understanding & Reading Conflicts
concepts.explanation: A conflict happens when Git can't automatically merge changes because two commits edited the same lines differently. Git marks the conflict in the file itself with special markers. Your job is to manually decide what the final code should look like.
concepts.code:
# Git marks conflicts like this in your file:
<<<<<<< HEAD                    ← Your changes (current branch)
const timeout = 5000;
=======                         ← Divider
const timeout = 3000;
>>>>>>> feature/faster-timeouts  ← Incoming changes

# To resolve:
# 1. Open the file, remove the markers
# 2. Decide what the final code should be:
const timeout = 3000;    // Keep incoming, or...
const timeout = 5000;    // Keep yours, or...
const timeout = 4000;    // Write something entirely new!

# 3. Stage the resolved file
git add src/config.js

# 4. Continue the merge
git commit  # Or: git merge --continue
keyPoints: <<<<<<< HEAD means YOUR code (the branch you're on)
>>>>>>> branch-name means THEIR code (the incoming branch)
======= divides your code from theirs
You MUST delete all three marker lines as part of resolution

concepts.name: Resolving Conflicts Workflow
concepts.explanation: A systematic approach to resolving conflicts without panic. The key is to understand BOTH sides of the conflict before deciding — read the context, understand the intent of each change, then craft the correct resolution.
concepts.code:
# Step 1: See which files have conflicts
git status
# Shows: both modified: src/api/auth.js

# Step 2: Use a merge tool (optional but helpful)
git mergetool          # Opens configured tool
git mergetool --tool=vscode

# Step 3: Edit the file manually
# Open the conflicted file, find all <<<<<<< markers
# Decide the correct final version

# Step 4: Verify you removed ALL conflict markers
grep -r '<<<<<<' src/   # Should return nothing!

# Step 5: Stage resolved files
git add src/api/auth.js

# Step 6: Complete the merge
git commit

# Useful: see what both sides changed
git diff --diff-filter=U   # Show only conflicted files
git log --merge --p        # Show commits that caused conflict
keyPoints: Don't rush conflict resolution — understand BOTH sides before deciding
git mergetool opens a 3-way diff: left = yours, right = theirs, middle = base
After resolving, always run your tests — conflicts in logic don't show as text conflicts
Set VS Code as merge tool: git config --global merge.tool vscode

concepts.name: Preventing Conflicts (Best Practices)
concepts.explanation: The best merge conflict is one that never happens. Small, frequent merges beat large, infrequent ones every time. These practices dramatically reduce conflict frequency and severity.
concepts.code:
# 1. Pull from main frequently
git fetch origin
git rebase origin/main   # (or: git merge origin/main)

# 2. Keep branches short-lived (< 2-3 days)

# 3. Communicate with team — announce you're editing a file

# 4. Split large files into smaller modules
# Conflicts cluster in big files touched by many people

# 5. Use git rerere to reuse previous conflict resolutions
git config --global rerere.enabled true
# Git remembers how you resolved a conflict pattern and auto-resolves it next time

# 6. See divergence before merging
git log main..feature --oneline  # Commits in feature not in main
git log feature..main --oneline  # Commits in main not in feature
git diff main...feature          # Changes that diverged
keyPoints: Sync with main at least daily on active features — the further you drift, the worse conflicts get
git rerere (reuse recorded resolution) is underused magic — enable it!
Conflicts in generated files (e.g., package-lock.json) are usually safe to take the latest version
Team coding standard in .editorconfig reduces whitespace conflicts

concepts.name: git restore — Discard Uncommitted Changes
concepts.explanation: You made changes in your working directory but want to throw them away and restore the file to how it was in the last commit. git restore (Git 2.23+) is the clean, purpose-built command for this. WARNING: this cannot be undone.
concepts.code:
# Discard changes in working directory (CANNOT UNDO)
git restore filename.js

# Discard all uncommitted changes in entire project
git restore .

# Unstage a file (remove from staging area, keep changes)
git restore --staged filename.js

# Unstage AND discard changes
git restore --staged --worktree filename.js

# Restore file from a specific commit
git restore --source abc1234 -- filename.js

# Restore file from another branch
git restore --source origin/main -- src/config.js
keyPoints: git restore . is DESTRUCTIVE — permanently throws away all uncommitted changes
--staged unstages without touching the file content — safe to use often
--source lets you restore any file from any commit or branch
The old way: git checkout -- filename.js (still works but confusing)

concepts.name: git reset — Move Branch Pointer
concepts.explanation: git reset moves the current branch pointer to a different commit. Three modes control how much is preserved. This is powerful for undoing local commits — but always dangerous on pushed branches because it rewrites history.
concepts.code:
# --soft: move pointer, keep changes STAGED
git reset --soft HEAD~1    # Undo last commit, keep staged
git reset --soft HEAD~3    # Undo last 3 commits, keep staged

# --mixed (default): move pointer, keep changes UNSTAGED
git reset HEAD~1           # Same as --mixed
git reset --mixed HEAD~2   # Undo 2 commits, unstage everything

# --hard: move pointer, DISCARD all changes
git reset --hard HEAD~1    # DANGER: lost! (unless in reflog)
git reset --hard origin/main  # Reset to match remote

# Use Cases:
# Undo last commit, keep edits for a new commit:
git reset --soft HEAD~1

# Completely start over from remote state:
git reset --hard origin/main
keyPoints: --soft: uncommit but keep staged — useful for combining commits or rewriting message
--mixed: uncommit and unstage — useful for splitting a commit into multiple
--hard: throw everything away — last resort, and only on local/unshared commits
HEAD~1 = one commit before HEAD; HEAD~3 = three commits before HEAD

concepts.name: git revert — Safe Undo on Public Branches
concepts.explanation: Unlike reset, git revert doesn't delete commits — it creates a NEW commit that is the opposite of the target commit. This is safe for public/shared branches because it doesn't rewrite history. Everyone can still see what happened.
concepts.code:
# Revert the most recent commit
git revert HEAD

# Revert a specific commit by SHA
git revert abc1234

# Revert multiple commits
git revert HEAD~3..HEAD

# Revert without auto-commit (stage the revert, you commit)
git revert -n abc1234
git commit -m "revert: undo payment API changes (broke invoices)"

# Revert a merge commit (need to specify parent)
git revert -m 1 merge_commit_sha
# -m 1 means: revert to first parent (typically main)
keyPoints: revert is ALWAYS safe on shared/public branches — it adds history, never removes it
reset is for LOCAL history cleanup; revert is for PUBLIC branch fixes
-m 1 for reverting merges: 1 = keep first parent (main), 2 = keep second parent (feature)
After revert, you can always cherry-pick the reverted commit back if it was a mistake

concepts.name: git reflog — Your Ultimate Safety Net
concepts.explanation: The reflog is Git's diary. It records EVERY position HEAD has been at, even after resets, rebases, and branch deletions. If you lost commits with git reset --hard or deleted a branch, reflog can get them back. It's local-only and kept for 90 days.
concepts.code:
# See the reflog (all recent HEAD positions)
git reflog
# Output:
# abc1234 HEAD@{0}: commit: feat: add search
# def5678 HEAD@{1}: reset: moving to HEAD~1
# ghi9012 HEAD@{2}: commit: WIP: half-done feature

# Recover a commit lost by git reset --hard
git reflog                 # Find the SHA you want
git switch -c recovery-branch HEAD@{2}  # Recover to new branch
# OR directly reset to it:
git reset --hard ghi9012

# Recover a deleted branch
git reflog                 # Find the last commit of that branch
git switch -c restored-branch abc1234

# See reflog for a specific branch
git reflog show feature/old-branch
keyPoints: reflog is LOCAL only — exists on your machine, not in remotes
Default retention is 90 days — act quickly after an accidental loss!
reflog entries look like HEAD@{n} — n=0 is the most recent position
This is why git reset --hard is recoverable locally but not on the remote

concepts.name: Removing Sensitive Data from History
concepts.explanation: Worst-case scenario: you committed a password, API key, or private key to Git (even to a private repo). Simply deleting the file and committing doesn't help — it's still in the history. You need to rewrite history.
concepts.code:
# Option 1: BFG Repo Cleaner (easiest, fast)
# Install: brew install bfg
bfg --replace-text passwords.txt my-repo.git
bfg --delete-files id_rsa my-repo.git
git reflog expire --expire=now --all
git gc --prune=now --aggressive
git push --force

# Option 2: git filter-repo (official, powerful)
# Install: pip install git-filter-repo
git filter-repo --path secrets.env --invert-paths
git filter-repo --replace-text expressions.txt

# Option 3: Legacy (slow, avoid if possible)
git filter-branch --force --index-filter \
  'git rm --cached --ignore-unmatch secrets.env' \
  --prune-empty --tag-name-filter cat -- --all

# CRITICAL: After any history rewrite, REVOKE the secret immediately!
# Assume the secret is compromised — treat it as stolen
keyPoints: FIRST ACTION: Rotate/revoke the exposed credential immediately — assume it was already seen
History rewrites require force pushing and all collaborators must re-clone
Use git-filter-repo (not filter-branch) — faster, safer, officially recommended
Use tools like git-secrets or pre-commit hooks to PREVENT committing secrets

concepts.name: Managing Remotes
concepts.explanation: A remote is just a URL with a nickname. 'origin' is the convention for the main remote (the repo you cloned from). You can have multiple remotes — useful for maintaining a fork alongside the upstream, or deploying to multiple services.
concepts.code:
# View remotes (name and URL)
git remote -v
# Output:
# origin  https://github.com/you/repo.git (fetch)
# origin  https://github.com/you/repo.git (push)

# Add a remote
git remote add upstream https://github.com/original/repo.git
git remote add heroku https://git.heroku.com/my-app.git

# Change remote URL (switch from HTTPS to SSH)
git remote set-url origin git@github.com:user/repo.git

# Rename a remote
git remote rename origin old-origin

# Remove a remote
git remote remove upstream

# Fetch without merging (inspect before integrating)
git fetch origin
git fetch --all   # Fetch from all remotes
keyPoints: origin is a convention, not a special name — you can rename it
upstream conventionally refers to the original repo you forked from
git fetch downloads but does NOT change your working files — safe to run anytime
Switch to SSH URL if tired of entering passwords: git remote set-url origin git@github.com:...

concepts.name: Fetching, Pulling & Pushing
concepts.explanation: The daily remote sync operations. fetch is inspect-only. pull is fetch + merge. push sends your commits up. Understanding the difference between fetch and pull prevents nasty surprises.
concepts.code:
# Fetch: download remote changes, don't touch working files
git fetch origin
git fetch --all

# After fetch: see what's new
git log HEAD..origin/main --oneline  # What remote has that local doesn't
git diff HEAD origin/main           # Diff between local and remote

# Pull: fetch + merge into current branch
git pull origin main

# Pull with rebase instead of merge (cleaner history)
git pull --rebase origin main
# Set as default:
git config --global pull.rebase true

# Push current branch
git push origin feature/my-feature

# Push and set upstream tracking in one step
git push -u origin feature/my-feature
# After -u, you can just type: git push

# Push all branches
git push --all origin

# Delete remote branch
git push origin --delete feature/old-branch
keyPoints: Prefer git pull --rebase to avoid unnecessary merge commits in local history
-u sets tracking so future git push/pull work without specifying branch
git push --force-with-lease instead of --force: safer because it checks if someone else pushed
You can only push to branches you have write access to

concepts.name: Tracking Branches & Upstream
concepts.explanation: A tracking branch is a local branch that knows which remote branch it corresponds to. This enables bare git push and git pull without specifying origin and branch name every time.
concepts.code:
# See tracking relationships for all branches
git branch -vv
# Output:
# * feature/search  abc123 [origin/feature/search] feat: add filters
#   main            def456 [origin/main] chore: release v2.1

# Set tracking on existing branch
git branch --set-upstream-to=origin/main main

# Push and set tracking at same time
git push -u origin feature/my-branch

# Check if your branch is ahead/behind remote
git status
# Output: Your branch is ahead of 'origin/main' by 2 commits.
#         Your branch is behind 'origin/main' by 3 commits.

# See compact ahead/behind for all branches
git branch -vv
keyPoints: Tracking = local branch knows its remote counterpart — enables parameterless push/pull
'ahead by N' = N commits you have locally that aren't pushed
'behind by N' = N commits on remote you haven't pulled yet
Always pull before pushing to avoid rejection and need for force-push

concepts.name: Creating a Perfect Pull Request
concepts.explanation: A good PR is a gift to reviewers. Small, focused, well-described. Bad PR: 47 files changed, 'misc fixes'. Good PR: one logical change with context, screenshots, and testing notes. The best PRs get reviewed and merged quickly.
concepts.code:
# PR preparation workflow:

# 1. Sync with latest main before opening PR
git fetch origin
git rebase origin/main   # Replay your commits on latest main

# 2. Clean up commit history
git rebase -i origin/main  # Interactive rebase from where you diverged
# Squash WIP commits, reword messages, reorder

# 3. Push to remote
git push -u origin feature/add-payment-gateway

# 4. In GitHub UI — write a great description:
# Title: "feat: add Stripe payment gateway integration"
# Body:
# ## What does this PR do?
# Integrates Stripe payments for checkout flow.
# ## How to test?
# 1. Add STRIPE_KEY to .env
# 2. Go to /checkout, use test card 4242 4242 4242 4242
# ## Screenshots
# [before/after screenshots]
# ## Related Issues
# Closes #142

# GitHub CLI alternative
gh pr create --title "feat: add payment gateway" --body "..."
keyPoints: Small PRs (<400 lines) get reviewed thoroughly; large PRs get rubber-stamped
'Closes #142' in PR body auto-closes the linked issue when PR merges
Attach screenshots for UI changes — saves reviewer from running the code
Self-review your diff first: git diff main..feature or the GitHub diff before requesting review

concepts.name: Code Review Best Practices
concepts.explanation: Code review is communication. The goal is better code, not proving you're smarter. Good reviews catch bugs, improve design, share knowledge, and maintain code standards. Tone matters as much as content.
concepts.code:
# For REVIEWERS:

# 1. Review the intent, not just the code
#    Ask: 'Does this actually solve the problem?'

# 2. Use the Conventional Comments standard:
#    praise:  Great use of early return here!
#    nitpick: nit: Could rename 'x' to 'userId'
#    issue:   This will throw NPE if user is null
#    question: q: Why 3 retries instead of 5?
#    thought:  thought: Could we use a Set here?

# 3. Be specific about required vs optional:
#    'blocking:' = must fix before merge
#    'nit:' = minor style, can ignore

# For PR AUTHORS:
# 1. Respond to ALL comments (even with just 'done' or 'ack')
# 2. If you disagree, explain why — don't silently ignore
# 3. Mark your own comments as resolved when addressed

# Checklist before requesting review:
# ✓ Does it do what the issue says?
# ✓ Are there tests?
# ✓ Does CI pass?
# ✓ Is the diff clean (no debug logs, no commented-out code)?
keyPoints: Nitpick (nit:) prefix signals 'this is optional, not blocking' — reduces friction
Approve with comments for minor nits; request changes only for actual issues
Reviews are asynchronous — be patient, set SLAs (e.g., review within 24 hours)
Check test coverage in the diff — new code without tests is the most common review comment

concepts.name: Merge Strategies for PRs
concepts.explanation: GitHub offers three merge strategies, each with different history implications. Teams should standardize on one strategy for consistency. Your choice affects how clean the git log looks on main.
concepts.code:
# Option 1: Create a merge commit (default)
# Preserves all commits + adds a merge commit
# Best: when you want full history of the feature branch
git merge --no-ff feature/add-auth
# main: ... A - B - M (M = merge commit)
#                    ↑
#                D - E (feature commits visible)

# Option 2: Squash and merge
# Combines ALL PR commits into ONE commit on main
# Best: when feature commits are messy WIP commits
git merge --squash feature/add-auth
git commit -m "feat: add user authentication"
# main: ... A - B - S (S = squash commit, clean)

# Option 3: Rebase and merge
# Replays commits linearly on main (no merge commit)
# Best: when commits are already clean and atomic
git rebase main && git merge --ff-only
# main: ... A - B - D' - E' (linear, no merge commit)
keyPoints: Squash merge is most popular in GitHub repos — clean main history, details in PR
Rebase merge gives linear history but makes it harder to see what was 'a feature'
Merge commit is best for release branches where you want to see complete feature scope
Set your team's strategy in repo settings: 'Allow squash merging' only for consistency

concepts.name: Working in a Team — Daily Workflow
concepts.explanation: Most team friction comes from people working in isolation too long, diverging from the main branch, and then trying to merge a week's worth of changes. The cure is simple: sync early, sync often, make small commits.
concepts.code:
# Morning routine — start the day synced
git fetch --all
git switch main
git pull origin main

# Switch to your feature branch and rebase
git switch feature/my-feature
git rebase origin/main

# If conflicts during rebase, resolve them:
# 1. Edit conflicted files
# 2. git add resolved-file.js
# 3. git rebase --continue

# Work through the day with atomic commits
git commit -m "feat: add cart model"
git commit -m "feat: wire cart to checkout API"
git commit -m "test: add cart unit tests"

# End of day: push your branch
git push origin feature/my-feature

# Check what teammates have pushed
git fetch origin
git log --oneline --all --graph -10
keyPoints: Rebase on main every morning — keeps your branch fresh and reduces conflict accumulation
Atomic commits: each commit makes one logical change and the tests pass
Push at end of every day — backs up your work and lets teammates see your progress
git fetch --all is always safe — never changes your working files

concepts.name: Handling Shared Feature Branches
concepts.explanation: When multiple developers work on the same feature branch, the usual rebase-happy workflow breaks down because you can't rewrite history that others have pulled. Switch to a merge-based approach for shared branches.
concepts.code:
# On a shared feature branch (multiple devs):

# 1. Always pull before pushing
git pull origin feature/shared-feature

# 2. Use merge (not rebase) to sync
git merge origin/feature/shared-feature
# NOT: git rebase origin/feature/shared-feature
# (rebasing would rewrite history your teammates have)

# 3. Push regularly to reduce divergence
git push origin feature/shared-feature

# 4. If push is rejected (someone else pushed):
git pull --no-rebase origin feature/shared-feature
git push origin feature/shared-feature

# 5. Communicate about who's editing what
#    Use GitHub's draft PR comments for coordination
keyPoints: Shared branches = use merge, never rebase
--no-rebase on pull does a merge pull (creates merge commits) — fine for shared branches
Avoid editing the same files simultaneously on shared branches — coordinate via standups/Slack
Consider creating personal sub-branches and merging into the shared feature branch via PRs

concepts.name: Keeping History Clean with Interactive Rebase
concepts.explanation: Before opening a PR, clean up your local branch history so reviewers see a logical progression of changes, not a trail of 'WIP fix typo oops fix again' commits. Interactive rebase lets you sculpt history into a professional narrative.
concepts.code:
# Interactive rebase on last N commits (replace N)
git rebase -i HEAD~5

# Or rebase from where you diverged from main:
git rebase -i origin/main

# The editor opens with something like:
# pick abc1234 feat: start login form
# pick def5678 WIP: half done
# pick ghi9012 fix typo
# pick jkl3456 actually fix the typo
# pick mno7890 feat: finish login validation

# Your goal: clean it up
# pick abc1234 feat: start login form
# squash def5678 WIP: half done       ← combine into pick above
# fixup ghi9012 fix typo              ← absorb, discard message
# fixup jkl3456 actually fix the typo ← absorb, discard message
# reword mno7890 feat: add user login validation  ← rename this

# Result: 2 clean commits instead of 5 messy ones
keyPoints: squash: combines into previous commit AND lets you edit the combined message
fixup: combines into previous commit, discards the message — faster for minor fixes
reword: keep the commit, just change the message
drop: removes the commit entirely — use carefully, changes are lost

concepts.name: The Fork & Pull Request Workflow
concepts.explanation: The standard way to contribute to open source projects. You fork (copy) the repository to your GitHub account, make changes, and submit a pull request back to the original. You never need write access to the original repo.
concepts.code:
# Step 1: Fork on GitHub (click Fork button in UI)

# Step 2: Clone YOUR fork
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/project.git
cd project

# Step 3: Add the ORIGINAL repo as upstream
git remote add upstream https://github.com/ORIGINAL/project.git
git remote -v
# origin   = your fork
# upstream = original repo

# Step 4: Create a feature branch (never work on main!)
git switch -c fix/typo-in-readme

# Step 5: Make changes, commit
git commit -m "docs: fix typo in installation section"

# Step 6: Push to YOUR fork
git push -u origin fix/typo-in-readme

# Step 7: Open PR on GitHub (it'll show 'Compare & pull request')
# Target: original/main ← your-fork/fix/typo-in-readme
keyPoints: Fork = your personal copy of the repo on GitHub (not a Git concept — it's a GitHub feature)
upstream = the original; origin = your fork — this naming is universal convention
Never commit directly to main on your fork — always use branches for PRs
GitHub's PR will automatically detect your fork and suggest the upstream as target

concepts.name: Keeping Your Fork Synced with Upstream
concepts.explanation: Forks don't auto-update. While you work on your fork, the original repo keeps getting new commits. You need to regularly pull from upstream to keep your fork current — especially before starting new work or PRs.
concepts.code:
# Fetch latest changes from the original repo
git fetch upstream

# Update your local main with upstream's main
git switch main
git merge upstream/main      # Merge upstream changes
# OR:
git rebase upstream/main     # Cleaner, but rewrites local commits

# Push updated main to YOUR fork on GitHub
git push origin main

# Keep your feature branch updated too
git switch fix/my-feature
git rebase upstream/main
git push --force-with-lease origin fix/my-feature

# One-liner to sync main:
git fetch upstream && git switch main && git merge upstream/main && git push origin main

# GitHub UI shortcut: Fork > Sync Fork button (added in 2022)
keyPoints: Always fetch upstream before starting new work on a fork
Your fork's main should be a mirror of upstream/main — no original commits on it
After rebasing a feature branch, force-push is required: git push --force-with-lease
A stale fork causes conflicts in your PRs — reviewers will ask you to rebase

concepts.name: Open Source Contribution Checklist
concepts.explanation: Contributing to open source has unwritten rules. Follow these practices to have your contribution accepted, not ignored or rejected. Maintainers are volunteers — make their job easy.
concepts.code:
# Before starting:
# ✓ Read CONTRIBUTING.md (exists in most repos)
# ✓ Search existing Issues to avoid duplicates
# ✓ Check if there's a related open Issue — comment on it
# ✓ If adding a feature, open an Issue first to discuss

# Setup:
git clone your-fork-url
git remote add upstream original-repo-url

# Coding:
# ✓ Follow the existing code style (or run: npm run lint)
# ✓ Add tests for new code
# ✓ Keep commits atomic and well-described
# ✓ Run the full test suite before pushing

# PR:
# ✓ Write a clear description of what and why
# ✓ Reference the related Issue: 'Fixes #42'
# ✓ Fill out the PR template if one exists
# ✓ Keep scope small — one problem per PR
# ✓ Be patient — maintainers are busy!

# After review:
# ✓ Respond to ALL comments
# ✓ Don't take feedback personally
# ✓ Make requested changes on the same branch (PR updates)
# ✓ Squash WIP commits before maintainer merges
keyPoints: CONTRIBUTING.md is the bible for that project — always read it first
Small PRs get merged; ambitious PRs get discussed forever. Start small.
Good first issues are labeled 'good first issue' — perfect starting point
Code of Conduct exists in most repos — it's binding, treat people with respect

concepts.name: GitHub CLI (gh) — GitHub in Your Terminal
concepts.explanation: The GitHub CLI brings GitHub's web UI features into your terminal. Create PRs, manage issues, check CI status — all without leaving your editor or browser. An underused productivity booster for developers who live in the terminal.
concepts.code:
# Install: brew install gh  (macOS)
# Windows: winget install GitHub.cli

# Authenticate
gh auth login

# Create a PR from current branch
gh pr create --title "feat: add search" --body "Adds fuzzy search"

# Create PR and open in browser
gh pr create --web

# List open PRs
gh pr list

# Check out a PR locally (for review)
gh pr checkout 42

# Check PR status and CI results
gh pr status
gh pr checks

# Merge a PR
gh pr merge 42 --squash

# Work with Issues
gh issue list
gh issue create
gh issue close 42

# View repo in browser
gh repo view --web

# Fork a repo and clone in one step
gh repo fork original/repo --clone
keyPoints: gh pr checkout 42 clones the PR branch locally — fastest way to review code
gh pr checks shows CI status — see if GitHub Actions passed without opening browser
Combine with scripts: gh pr list --json number,title | jq '.[] | .title'
gh can create releases, manage secrets, trigger workflows — check gh --help

concepts.name: GitHub Actions — Automated CI/CD
concepts.explanation: GitHub Actions runs automated workflows on every push, PR, or schedule. Use it for CI (run tests on every PR), CD (deploy on merge to main), linting, security scanning, and more. Lives in .github/workflows/.
concepts.code:
# File: .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Run tests
        run: npm test

      - name: Run linter
        run: npm run lint

# cd.yml — Deploy on push to main
name: Deploy
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: ./scripts/deploy.sh
keyPoints: Workflows are YAML files in .github/workflows/ — commit them like regular code
uses: actions/checkout@v4 clones your repo into the CI runner
Secrets like API keys go in Settings > Secrets — access as ${{ secrets.MY_KEY }}
Matrix builds let you test on multiple OS/Node versions in parallel

concepts.name: Branch Protection Rules
concepts.explanation: Branch protection rules prevent accidental (or intentional) direct pushes to important branches. Require PRs, passing tests, and code reviews before anything merges to main. Essential for any serious project.
concepts.code:
# Set up in: GitHub > Repo Settings > Branches > Add rule

# Essential rules to enable for 'main':

# ✓ Require a pull request before merging
#   - Required approvals: 1 (or 2 for teams)
#   - Dismiss stale reviews on new push

# ✓ Require status checks to pass before merging
#   - Select your CI workflow (e.g., 'test')
#   - Require branches to be up to date before merging

# ✓ Require conversation resolution before merging

# ✓ Include administrators (no bypassing for anyone!)

# ✓ Restrict who can push directly to main

# Effect of these rules:
# git push origin main  → REJECTED with error:
# ! [remote rejected] main -> main (protected branch hook declined)
# You MUST open a PR and get it approved first
keyPoints: 'Require branches to be up to date' forces PR authors to rebase on main before merge
'Dismiss stale reviews' means approval is reset when new commits are pushed — re-review required
Include administrators prevents the 'I'll just push directly since I'm the owner' trap
Use CODEOWNERS file to automatically assign reviewers based on file paths

concepts.name: GitHub Issues & Project Management
concepts.explanation: GitHub Issues are more than bug reports — they're the unit of work. Combined with Projects boards, milestones, and labels, they become a lightweight but powerful project management system that lives alongside your code.
concepts.code:
# Issue best practices:
# Good title: "Login form crashes when email contains +"
# Bad title:  "Login broken"

# Issue templates: .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/bug_report.md
# ---
# name: Bug Report
# about: Create a report to help us improve
# ---
# **Describe the bug**
# **Steps to reproduce**
# **Expected behavior**
# **Environment**

# Linking commits and PRs to issues:
git commit -m "fix: handle special chars in email (fixes #42)"
# Keywords: closes, fixes, resolves → auto-close issue on merge

# Labels for organization:
# bug, enhancement, good first issue, help wanted,
# priority: high, status: in-progress

# Milestones: group issues by release/sprint
# v2.0, Sprint 3, Q1 2025

# GitHub Projects: Kanban board linked to issues
# Todo → In Progress → In Review → Done
keyPoints: Close keywords (closes, fixes, resolves) in PR description auto-close the issue on merge
'good first issue' label is your tool for attracting contributors to open source projects
GitHub Projects v2 has roadmap view, custom fields, and workflow automation
Issue templates (.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/) ensure contributors provide the right info

concepts.name: git blame — Find Who Changed a Line
concepts.explanation: git blame shows you who last modified every line of a file and in which commit. Not for assigning blame (not productive!) — for finding context: which commit introduced this code, what was the reasoning behind it?
concepts.code:
# Show who changed each line of a file
git blame src/api/payment.js

# Output:
# abc123 (Alice  2024-01-15) const processPayment = (amount) => {
# def456 (Bob    2024-01-18)   if (amount <= 0) throw new Error('Invalid');
# abc123 (Alice  2024-01-15)   return stripe.charge(amount);

# Ignore whitespace changes in blame
git blame -w src/api/payment.js

# Show blame for a specific range of lines
git blame -L 50,75 src/api/payment.js

# Show blame at a specific commit (time travel)
git blame HEAD~5 -- src/api/payment.js

# Better blame in VS Code: GitLens extension
# Hover over any line → shows commit + PR + Jira ticket

# After finding the commit, get full details:
git show abc123
git log --oneline -1 abc123
keyPoints: git blame is for understanding code history, not criticizing teammates
-w ignores whitespace-only changes (formatting commits) — more accurate blame
-L start,end limits to a line range — useful for large files
git show COMMIT shows the full diff for that commit — the commit message explains WHY

concepts.name: git bisect — Binary Search for Bugs
concepts.explanation: A bug was introduced somewhere in the last 200 commits. How do you find which one? git bisect performs a binary search through your commit history — you mark commits as good or bad, and Git finds the exact offending commit in O(log n) steps.
concepts.code:
# Start bisect session
git bisect start

# Mark current commit as bad (bug exists)
git bisect bad

# Mark a known good commit (before bug existed)
git bisect good v1.5.0          # tag
git bisect good abc1234         # SHA
git bisect good HEAD~50         # 50 commits ago

# Git checks out the middle commit — test it
# Does the bug exist? → git bisect bad
# No bug? → git bisect good
# Git narrows down and checks out next candidate

# Repeat until Git prints:
# abc1234 is the first bad commit
# author: Alice, date: 2024-03-05
# feat: refactor payment processor

# Show the changes in the culprit commit
git show abc1234

# End bisect session (back to original branch)
git bisect reset

# Automate with a script
git bisect run npm test   # Auto-bisect: bad=test fails, good=test passes
keyPoints: Binary search means 200 commits ≈ only 8 steps — incredibly fast bug hunting
git bisect run <command> fully automates: command exit 0 = good, non-zero = bad
Always run git bisect reset when done — otherwise you're in a detached HEAD state
Works best when you have a test that exposes the bug: git bisect run ./test-bug.sh

concepts.name: Searching Code History (git log + grep)
concepts.explanation: Answer questions like: 'When was this function added?', 'Who deleted the caching logic?', 'What changed this error message?' — using Git's powerful history search capabilities.
concepts.code:
# Find commits that added or removed a specific string
git log -S 'processPayment' --oneline
# -S = 'pickaxe' — finds commits where count of string changed

# Find commits that changed a string with regex
git log -G 'function.*processPayment' --oneline
# -G = regex match in the diff content

# Search commit MESSAGES
git log --grep='bug fix' --oneline
git log --grep='JIRA-4521' --oneline

# Find all commits affecting a file
git log --oneline -- src/payment/processor.js

# See what ACTUALLY changed in those commits
git log -p -S 'processPayment'

# When was a specific function introduced?
git log -S 'function calculateTax' --oneline

# What was in a file 6 months ago?
git show HEAD@{6.months.ago}:src/config.js
keyPoints: -S (pickaxe) finds commits where the occurrence count of the string changes — additions or deletions
-G uses regex on the entire diff content — more flexible than -S
--grep searches commit MESSAGES; -S/-G search diff CONTENT
HEAD@{date} lets you view file state at any point in time: git show HEAD@{2024-01-01}:filename

concepts.name: Git Tags & Semantic Versioning
concepts.explanation: Tags mark specific commits as release points. While branches move forward, tags are permanent markers — like pinning a bookmark at a specific point in history. Semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH) is the universal standard.
concepts.code:
# Semantic versioning:
# v1.2.3
# MAJOR: breaking changes (v1 → v2)
# MINOR: new features, backward-compatible (v1.1 → v1.2)
# PATCH: bug fixes (v1.2.0 → v1.2.1)

# Create a lightweight tag (just a pointer)
git tag v1.2.3

# Create an annotated tag (PREFERRED — has metadata)
git tag -a v1.2.3 -m "Release v1.2.3: add payment gateway"

# Tag a specific past commit
git tag -a v1.2.3 abc1234 -m "Release v1.2.3"

# List all tags
git tag
git tag -l 'v1.*'  # Filter with pattern

# See tag details
git show v1.2.3

# Push tags to remote (tags are NOT pushed by default!)
git push origin v1.2.3     # Push specific tag
git push origin --tags     # Push all local tags

# Delete tag
git tag -d v1.2.3          # Local
git push origin --delete v1.2.3  # Remote
keyPoints: Annotated tags (-a) include author, date, message — always prefer over lightweight tags
Tags are NOT pushed with git push — explicitly push with --tags or by tag name
GitHub auto-creates releases from tags — add release notes in the GitHub UI
Pre-release versions: v2.0.0-alpha.1, v2.0.0-beta.2, v2.0.0-rc.1

concepts.name: Creating GitHub Releases
concepts.explanation: GitHub Releases turn tags into published release entries with release notes, attached binaries, and changelogs. This is what users see when they download a specific version of your software.
concepts.code:
# Via GitHub CLI:
gh release create v1.2.3 \
  --title "v1.2.3 — Payment Gateway Integration" \
  --notes "## What's new
- Added Stripe payment processing
- Fixed edge case in cart total calculation
- Performance improvements in checkout flow

## Breaking Changes
None

## Upgrade Guide
Update .env with STRIPE_KEY" \
  dist/app-linux.tar.gz dist/app-macos.tar.gz

# Via GitHub UI:
# Releases > Draft a new release > Choose tag

# Auto-generate changelog from PR descriptions:
gh release create v1.2.3 --generate-notes

# Create a pre-release
gh release create v2.0.0-beta.1 --prerelease

# Automate with GitHub Actions on tag push:
# on:
#   push:
#     tags: ['v*']
# steps:
#   - run: gh release create ${{ github.ref_name }} --generate-notes
keyPoints: GitHub auto-generates changelog from PR titles since last release with --generate-notes
Attach build artifacts (binaries, .zip, .jar) directly to the release for distribution
Pre-releases are visible on the releases page but not shown as 'latest'
Use CHANGELOG.md (conventional-changelog format) for a human-curated history

concepts.name: Accidentally Pushed to Main
concepts.explanation: Someone pushed commits directly to main that shouldn't be there (or pushed broken code). This is one of the most common Git emergencies in teams. Here are your recovery options from safest to most drastic.
concepts.code:
# Scenario: bad commits pushed to main
# Option A (SAFEST): revert the commits (preserves history)
git revert HEAD          # Revert last commit
git revert abc123 def456 # Revert specific commits
git push origin main

# Option B: Reset AND force push (rewrites history)
# WARN: Only if main is not protected and team is aware!
git reset --hard HEAD~2  # Undo last 2 commits locally
git push --force-with-lease origin main
# Everyone on the team must: git fetch && git reset --hard origin/main

# Option C: If main is protected (can't push directly)
# 1. Create a revert PR
# 2. Get approval
# 3. Merge revert PR

# Check what you're reverting first!
git log --oneline -5
git show HEAD              # See what the bad commit did
keyPoints: Revert is ALWAYS the safe option for shared branches — it adds history, never deletes it
Force push to main is drastic — teammates must resync their local repos afterward
--force-with-lease is safer than --force: fails if remote has extra commits you haven't seen
After any force push, broadcast immediately: 'HEADS UP: force pushed to main, please git fetch && git reset --hard origin/main'

concepts.name: Lost Commits After git reset --hard
concepts.explanation: You ran git reset --hard and thought you lost your work. Good news: Git almost never actually deletes commits immediately. The reflog has them for 90 days. Here's how to recover.
concepts.code:
# Step 1: Don't panic. Check the reflog immediately.
git reflog
# Find entry: abc1234 HEAD@{3}: commit: feat: payment integration

# Step 2: Check what that commit has
git show abc1234

# Step 3: Recover by creating a new branch from it
git switch -c recover/payment-integration HEAD@{3}
# Or: git switch -c recovery abc1234

# Step 4: Verify the work is there
git log --oneline -5

# Step 5: Now safely merge/cherry-pick back to original branch
git switch main
git merge recover/payment-integration

# Alternative: just reset to the recovered position
git reset --hard abc1234
keyPoints: git reset --hard only removes the branch pointer reference — the commit data stays for 90 days
reflog is the key — it records every position HEAD has been at
Create a recovery branch from the reflog entry — safer than directly resetting again
This only works LOCALLY — commits never pushed are unrecoverable from the remote

concepts.name: Accidentally Deleted a Branch
concepts.explanation: You deleted a branch that wasn't fully merged yet. The commits are still there — the branch was just a pointer. Use the reflog to find where the branch's HEAD was and recreate it.
concepts.code:
# Find the last commit on the deleted branch
git reflog
# Look for: "checkout: moving from deleted-branch to main"
# The commit BEFORE that checkout is the branch tip

# Or search by commit message pattern
git log --walk-reflogs --all --oneline | grep 'feature-name'

# Recreate the branch at that commit
git switch -c feature/restored-branch abc1234

# Verify it's all there
git log --oneline -10

# PREVENTION: Before deleting, check it's merged
git branch --merged main  # Only shows merged branches
git branch -d safeName    # -d (lowercase) refuses to delete unmerged

# GitHub tip: GitHub keeps deleted branches for 30 days!
# Merged PR > 'Restore branch' button appears for 30 days
keyPoints: GitHub keeps deleted branches for 30 days after PR merge — use 'Restore branch' button
git branch -d (lowercase) protects you: refuses to delete unmerged branches
git log --walk-reflogs searches through all reflog entries — great for finding lost branches
For remote branches: git fetch origin --prune syncs local tracking refs after remote deletions

concepts.name: Fix a Broken Merge Attempt
concepts.explanation: A merge or rebase went sideways. Conflicts everywhere, editor open, you're not sure what state things are in. These are the abort commands that get you back to a clean state so you can start over.
concepts.code:
# Abort an in-progress merge
git merge --abort

# Abort an in-progress rebase
git rebase --abort

# Abort an in-progress cherry-pick
git cherry-pick --abort

# Abort an in-progress revert
git revert --abort

# What state am I in? (shows if merge/rebase/cherry-pick is in progress)
git status
# Look for: 'You are currently rebasing...' or
#           'You have unmerged paths'

# Nuclear option: discard ALL changes, get back to last commit
git reset --hard HEAD
git clean -fd  # Remove untracked files and directories

# Start merge fresh with a specific strategy
git merge -X ours feature/branch   # Prefer our version in conflicts
git merge -X theirs feature/branch # Prefer their version
keyPoints: --abort commands are always safe — they restore the pre-operation state completely
git status always tells you your current Git state — read it carefully in emergencies
git clean -fd removes untracked files (like generated conflict backups) — DESTRUCTIVE
-X ours/-X theirs auto-resolves conflicts in bulk — useful when you're confident in one side

concepts.name: Recovering from Broken Repository
concepts.explanation: In rare cases, a repo can get into a corrupt or stuck state. These are the diagnostics and fixes for when nothing makes sense and normal commands fail.
concepts.code:
# Diagnose repository problems
git fsck
# Checks object database for corruption

# Clean up unnecessary files and optimize
git gc

# Remove leftover lock files (stops 'Another git process is running')
rm -f .git/index.lock
rm -f .git/MERGE_HEAD
rm -f .git/MERGE_MSG
rm -f .git/CHERRY_PICK_HEAD
rm -f .git/REBASE_HEAD

# Stuck merge state? Force-clear it:
git merge --abort 2>/dev/null; git rebase --abort 2>/dev/null

# Broken index? Rebuild it:
git rm -r --cached .
git add .

# Worst case: re-clone but keep your uncommitted changes
git stash
cd ..
git clone <url> project-fresh
cd project-fresh
git stash pop  # (from original repo's stash — moves with you)
keyPoints: .git/index.lock means another Git process is running (or crashed) — safe to delete if no Git process active
git fsck --lost-found writes orphaned commit objects to .git/lost-found/ — recoverable
git gc compresses history and removes unreferenced objects — safe and recommended monthly
When all else fails: stash, reclone, apply stash — you lose local history but keep code

concepts.name: Writing Great Commit Messages
concepts.explanation: A commit message is a message to the future — to your teammates, to yourself 6 months from now, to the automated tools parsing your history. The Conventional Commits standard has become the industry default for structured, readable commit messages.
concepts.code:
# Conventional Commits format:
# <type>(<scope>): <short description>
# [blank line]
# [optional body]
# [blank line]
# [optional footer]

# Types:
# feat:     new feature
# fix:      bug fix
# docs:     documentation only
# style:    formatting, no logic change
# refactor: code restructure, no behavior change
# test:     adding or fixing tests
# chore:    build tools, dependencies, CI
# perf:     performance improvement
# revert:   reverts a previous commit

# Examples:
git commit -m "feat(auth): add JWT token refresh mechanism"
git commit -m "fix(cart): calculate total correctly for discount codes"
git commit -m "docs: update README with Docker setup instructions"
git commit -m "chore: upgrade axios from 1.3 to 1.6"

# With body (use: git commit, not -m):
# fix(payment): handle zero-amount transactions
#
# Previously, Stripe API was called with amount=0 which
# caused an error. Now we validate before calling.
#
# Closes #234
keyPoints: Subject line: max 72 chars, imperative mood ('add' not 'added'), no period at end
Body explains the WHY, not the what — the diff already shows what changed
Tools like semantic-release and auto-changelog use Conventional Commits to automate versioning
'BREAKING CHANGE:' in the footer triggers a major version bump in semantic-release

concepts.name: Git Aliases — Speed Up Your Workflow
concepts.explanation: Git aliases are custom shortcuts for commands you type 50 times a day. Setting up a handful of power aliases transforms your Git experience. These are the aliases that professional developers swear by.
concepts.code:
# Set up globally useful aliases
git config --global alias.st status
git config --global alias.co checkout
git config --global alias.sw switch
git config --global alias.br branch
git config --global alias.cm 'commit -m'
git config --global alias.ca 'commit -am'

# The must-have one-line log
git config --global alias.lg "log --oneline --graph --all --decorate -20"

# See what you changed today
git config --global alias.today "log --oneline --since='midnight'"

# Undo last commit (keep changes staged)
git config --global alias.undo 'reset --soft HEAD~1'

# Clean merged branches
git config --global alias.cleanup '!git branch --merged main | grep -v main | xargs git branch -d'

# Now use them:
git st           # git status
git lg           # Pretty graph log
git undo         # Undo last commit, keep staged
git cm "fix: typo" # git commit -m
git cleanup      # Delete merged branches
keyPoints: ! prefix means shell command — enables powerful aliases that chain git commands
Aliases live in ~/.gitconfig — you can edit the file directly instead of using config commands
Share aliases via dotfiles repo — consistent experience across machines
git lg is the single most useful alias — pretty-print the commit graph

concepts.name: Pre-commit Hooks — Automate Code Quality
concepts.explanation: Git hooks are scripts that run automatically at specific points in your Git workflow. Pre-commit hooks run before every commit — perfect for catching lint errors, test failures, and secrets before they get into the codebase.
concepts.code:
# Option A: Manual hook (no extra tools)
# Create: .git/hooks/pre-commit
#!/bin/sh
npm run lint
if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
  echo 'Linting failed. Commit blocked.'
  exit 1
fi

# Option B: Husky (shareable, committed to repo)
npm install --save-dev husky lint-staged
npx husky init

# .husky/pre-commit:
#!/bin/sh
npx lint-staged

# package.json:
# "lint-staged": {
#   "*.{js,ts}": ["eslint --fix", "prettier --write"],
#   "*.{css,scss}": ["stylelint --fix"]
# }

# Option C: pre-commit (Python-based, universal)
# pip install pre-commit
# .pre-commit-config.yaml:
# repos:
#   - repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
#     rev: v4.5.0
#     hooks:
#       - id: detect-private-key
#       - id: check-added-large-files
#       - id: end-of-file-fixer
keyPoints: Hooks in .git/hooks/ are not committed — Husky commits hooks to the repo so the whole team uses them
lint-staged only lints STAGED files — fast because it doesn't lint the entire codebase
detect-private-key hook prevents committing SSH keys and credentials
Hooks can be bypassed with git commit --no-verify — for extreme emergencies only

concepts.name: Monorepo Git Strategies
concepts.explanation: Managing multiple packages or services in a single repository (monorepo). Git itself is package-agnostic, but you need strategies for selective commits, managing history per package, and efficient CI.
concepts.code:
# Git sparse checkout — only checkout specific directories
git clone --filter=blob:none --sparse https://github.com/org/monorepo
git sparse-checkout set services/payment packages/shared

# Verify what's checked out
git sparse-checkout list

# Partial clone — skip large files until needed
git clone --filter=blob:none https://github.com/org/monorepo

# Trigger CI only for changed packages (GitHub Actions)
jobs:
  changes:
    outputs:
      payment: ${{ steps.filter.outputs.payment }}
    steps:
      - uses: dorny/paths-filter@v3
        id: filter
        with:
          filters: |
            payment:
              - 'services/payment/**'
  test-payment:
    needs: changes
    if: ${{ needs.changes.outputs.payment == 'true' }}
    steps:
      - run: cd services/payment && npm test

# Useful git log for specific package:
git log --oneline -- services/payment/
keyPoints: Sparse checkout reduces checkout size in large monorepos — only get what you need
--filter=blob:none makes a partial clone: metadata downloaded now, files on demand
Path filtering in CI prevents running all tests when only one service changed
git log -- path limits history to changes affecting that directory

concepts.name: Git Config Recommendations for Teams
concepts.explanation: A set of Git configurations that every team should standardize on to prevent common frustrations — line ending conflicts, incorrect merge strategies, suboptimal diff algorithms, and unsafe push defaults.
concepts.code:
# Recommended global config for all developers on your team

# Safer push default (push only current branch)
git config --global push.default current

# Always pull with rebase (cleaner history)
git config --global pull.rebase true

# Auto-stash before rebase (saves mid-work state)
git config --global rebase.autoStash true

# Reuse recorded resolutions (auto-resolve seen conflicts)
git config --global rerere.enabled true

# Better diff algorithm (more human-readable diffs)
git config --global diff.algorithm histogram

# Line ending handling (critical for Windows/Mac mixed teams)
# Windows:
git config --global core.autocrlf true
# Mac/Linux:
git config --global core.autocrlf input

# Better merge conflict style (shows common ancestor too)
git config --global merge.conflictstyle zdiff3

# Case-sensitive filesystem (important on Mac/Linux)
git config --global core.ignorecase false

# Verify your full config
git config --list --global
keyPoints: push.default current is safer than 'matching' — only pushes active branch, avoids surprises
rebase.autoStash stashes uncommitted changes before rebase and re-applies — prevents 'cannot rebase dirty index' errors
merge.conflictstyle zdiff3 adds the common ancestor to conflict markers — easier resolution
Create a team .gitconfig template and document it in your onboarding guide
